reasoning: Peer set exhibits dense, high-craft pixel art with cool-to-neutral, low-saturation palettes and hard-stepped detail work — stone textures, cracks, object overlays, tight value contrast. The candidate is a flat, near-featureless bright-green field with scattered orange-dot noise. (1) Palette overlap: near zero — peers live in slate-gray/dark-brown/black; candidate is saturated lime-green with orange accents, brightness delta is enormous. (2) Pixel density: peers pack 20-40 distinct features per 32x32 cell; candidate has <5 non-noise pixels per cell — density ratio roughly 10:1 deficit. (3) Shading: peers use deliberate hard-stepped multi-value shading; candidate is essentially single-value flat fill with random 1px noise — no edge lighting, no depth cues. (4) Warmth/saturation: peers are cool and desaturated; candidate is over-saturated warm green — feels like a placeholder or prototype block. (5) Hand-pixeled charm: peers show clear intentional craft; candidate reads as procedural noise generation, not authored pixel art. (6) Photo-realism: both zero — neutral. (7) Overall feel: peers communicate a cohesive classic RPG dungeon/outdoor world with strong visual grammar; candidate looks like a Minecraft default block or a tileset from a completely different, much simpler game. Allowing fully for surface-type differences (stone vs grass), the rendering style, value range, density, and craft level are all severe mismatches. Cannot pass without a complete rework to match peer pixel density and value/saturation discipline.
